text: The dominant discourse on globalization, international inequality and development is still focused on economic growth. As growth as the goal of development has come under increasing criticism due to its socially and ecologically destructive consequences – not least because the increase in global GDP is often seen as the driving force behind the consumption of resources and the disruption of global life support systems – a number of postgrowth theories have been developed in various forms. This article provides a comparative analysis of three of them, inquiring into their intellectual history, development and impact on global policy, in order to gain insights into their potential for contesting globalization as a growth-based project and for shaping the international development agenda.
